title: Couching on felts
subtitle: Transferring freshly pulled sheets for pressing and drying
excerpt: Couching is the moment the sheet leaves the mould. The freshly pulled layer of wet fibre is rolled off the mould face and onto a damp couching cloth, where it will be pressed and then dried. The quality of the couch determines whether the sheet releases cleanly and lies flat, or folds, tears, or sticks.
sourceNotes: Couching technique synthesised from general hand-papermaking practice. The motion and felt-preparation described here are consistent with the Western hand-papermaking revival tradition.

glossaryTerms.definition: Transferring a freshly pulled wet sheet from the mould face onto a damp couching cloth by pressing the mould face-down and rolling it across the cloth. Pronounced 'COO-ching' in the papermaking tradition.
In traditional hand papermaking, a stack of alternating wet sheets and couching felts ready for pressing. A post of ten to twenty sheets is pressed all at once.

content.text: motion is one of the skills in hand papermaking that feels counterintuitive the first time but becomes natural with practice. You are transferring a fragile, waterlogged layer of fibre from one surface to another without tearing it. The key is a damp couching cloth, a controlled rolling motion, and enough confidence to commit to the lift.

The couching cloth must be damp but not wet. Soak it briefly, then wring it out firmly. Lay it completely flat on the press board, with no wrinkles. A wrinkle under the cloth will cause a ridge in the finished sheet that is difficult to remove even with pressing. If you are building a
of multiple sheets, wet each cloth to the same dampness before laying down the next couch.

Hold the mould (with the deckle removed) at a shallow angle, draining side up, with the near edge about 20 mm from the near edge of the couching cloth. In a single continuous motion, press the near edge of the mould face firmly onto the cloth and roll the mould forward, bringing the far edge down. The contact line moves from near to far across the cloth without lifting. Maintain even pressure across the full width. Lift the mould away cleanly by peeling the far edge up first.

If the sheet does not release cleanly, the most likely causes are: the couching cloth is too dry; the mould mesh has a fibre or debris stuck in it from a previous pull; or the pulp is too dilute and the sheet has very little structural integrity. Dampen the cloth, clean the mesh, or add more pulp to the vat respectively.

Place the second press board on top of the last couching cloth. Weight the stack with heavy books, or use the book press if you have one. Press firmly for 20, 30 minutes. The goal is to remove as much water as possible before air-drying. Under-pressed sheets dry slowly and may cockle; well-pressed sheets lift cleanly from the cloth and dry flatter.

After pressing, lift the top press board. The cloths and sheets will be damp but no longer saturated. Peel each cloth back carefully from its corner: the sheet will either stay on the cloth (peel the cloth away from the sheet) or stay on the board below (lift the cloth away upward). Separate gently. Do not rush the separation: a too-quick pull on a thin sheet will tear it.
